sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Acute coronary syndrome (ACS) represents a myriad of clinical presentations sharing common symptoms, ranging from worsening angina through to ST-elevation MI (STEMI).
Most patients present with prolonged (>10mins) or recurrent central or left sided chest discomfort described as tightness, heaviness, squeezing or crushing. The absence of these symptoms does not rule out ACS. Patients suffering from Acute Coronary Syndromes do not always present with chest pain and are subsequently frequently misdiagnosed.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Signs & Symptoms Suggestive of ACS:**
- Chest pain or discomfort of presumed cardiac origin (pressure, tightness, heaviness, or burning sensation)
- Acute or subacute shortness of breath without clear alternative explanation (notably, up to one-third of ACS patients may present without chest pain)
- Referred pain, i.e., to the jaw/teeth, interscapular pain (pain between the shoulder blades), epigastric pain or discomfort
- Unexplained upper limb or neck discomfort (especially in female patients)
- Dizziness, light-headedness, or transient loss of consciousness
- Nausea, vomiting, or diaphoresis (excessive sweating) without obvious cause
- Palpitations or dysrhythmias (abnormal heart rhythms)
- Generalized weakness or malaise
- Right-sided chest pain (less common but possible)
- Feeling of impending doom

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Suspected ACS should be judged from the history, risk profile, examination, and serial ECG findings rather than any single feature
- A normal or initially non-diagnostic ECG does not exclude ACS
- Repeat 12 lead ECGs are recommended in patients with ongoing symptoms, recurrent pain, or high clinical suspicion
- If the first ECG is non-diagnostic and symptoms continue, repeat ECGs at least every 15 minutes where feasible
- Early defibrillator pad placement is reasonable for confirmed STEMI or patients felt to be at high risk of deterioration
- Inferior or right ventricular STEMI may be associated with hypotension and bradycardia; avoid GTN unless haemodynamically appropriate and discussed with the on-call doctor when there is concern for right ventricular involvement
- GTN may precipitate hypotension in preload-dependent patients or in those who have recently used phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors
- Patients with diabetes, older age, or female sex may have atypical ACS presentations
- Spontaneously resolving ischaemic pain still warrants hospital evaluation
- If reperfusion or urgent specialist review may be indicated, minimise delays and organise rapid transport or evacuation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Continuous reassurance and minimise patient exertion
- Administer oxygen only if the patient is hypoxaemic, in respiratory distress, or has features of heart failure or shock; titrate to a target SpO2 of 94–98%
- Do not give oxygen routinely to patients with normal oxygen saturation
- Perform and repeat vital signs, including pain scores before and after intervention
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG where trained to do so and repeat if symptoms continue or recur
- If STEMI or STEMI equivalent is suspected, consider early defibrillation pad placement
- Send ECG to the on-call doctor for review where available
- Administer aspirin 300 mg as early as possible unless contraindicated
- Consider GTN only if blood pressure and clinical context are suitable
- Consider Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Repeat or extended 12 lead ECG assessment as clinically indicated, including right-sided or posterior leads when relevant
- Gain vascular access
- Consider appropriate analgesia, usually fentanyl titrated to effect in consultation with the on-call doctor when required
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Consider small fluid boluses if hypotensive and right ventricular involvement is suspected
- Anticoagulation decisions such as enoxaparin should be made under local protocol and senior medical direction rather than used routinely from this guideline alone

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- ESO’s should hold a low threshold for requesting further medical support
- The terminology used in this cohort of presentations continues to change with STEMI also known as STEACS (ST segment elevation acute coronary syndrome) and NSTEACS (non- ST segment elevation acute coronary syndrome)
